summary:
Trajan's Column (completed 113 AD, Rome) is one of the most extraordinary works of ancient art and one of history's most valuable historical documents — a 30-meter marble column whose helical band of relief carvings depicts in extraordinary detail the two [[event:2113|Dacian Wars]] of Emperor Trajan. The frieze, approximately 200 meters long if unrolled, contains over 2,500 figures and scenes depicting the Roman army crossing the Danube on pontoon bridges, constructing camps, conducting sieges, fighting battles, and receiving the surrender of Dacian chiefs — a visual encyclopedia of Roman military practice of the 2nd century AD.

The column served multiple purposes: a funerary monument (Trajan's ashes were interred in the base), a visual propaganda record of his greatest military achievement, a symbol of Roman power visible throughout the Forum of Trajan, and possibly a viewable artwork from surrounding buildings. The narrative technique — continuous frieze with no frames separating scenes — and the extraordinary level of detail in depicting weapons, armor, military equipment, camp construction, siege machinery, and soldier interactions have made Trajan's Column the single most important source for Roman military history. The column was so respected that it was never destroyed (unlike nearly all other Roman monuments) and stands intact in Rome today. Pope Sixtus V placed a bronze statue of St. Peter at the top in 1588, replacing Trajan's original (lost) statue.
